originalTitle: Switzerland knock out Colombia on penalties to reach World Cup quarter-finals
summary: After a goalless draw with Colombia, Switzerland won a penalty shootout 4-3 with Ruben Vargas converting the winning spot-kick to set up a match against Argentina
content: Davinson Sánchez looked to the heavens. Cucho Hernández trudged back to his teammates. And by the end, the rest of the Colombian team were on the grass as Switzerland danced, nearly alone in a sea of yellow. Switzerland had prevailed on penalties, 4-3, bringing an emotional end to more than two hours of tense, tentative, and ludicrously goal-free football in the World Cup last 16. Switzerland advance to the quarter-finals for the first time since 1954, when that stage was the first in the knockout round of a Swiss-hosted tournament featuring a total of 16 teams. They will face a tall task to better that result, facing Lionel Messi and Argentina in Kansas City in four days’ time. More chess game than football match, Colombia and Switzerland probed and prodded equally for more than 120 minutes, each searching for an elusive breakthrough. Possession remained even. Midfields took turns controlling proceedings, but only for minutes at a time. Sometimes those midfields were cut out entirely as long balls were traded to test capable backlines. The occasion lacked fireworks, but there was plenty of drama at the end. The game could surely have benefited from the finishing touch of the Swiss breakout star Johan Manzambi, their leading scorer who sustained a knee injury in training and was not on the team sheet. Colombia continued to run their attack through James Rodríguez. The distinctive roof of BC Place remained closed on a warm and sunny day in British Columbia, with an air conditioning system that seemed slightly overmatched. A thick humidity permeated the air in the final game at this venue and in Canada as a whole, closing the co‑host nation’s involvement in this tournament. The conditions did nothing to dampen the boisterous crowd, and there was no question about which side they were here to see. The vast majority of the 52,497 sellout gathering wore some version of bright Colombian yellow, singing throughout and jeering whenever their side were out of possession. Barranquilla this was not, but it was about as close as you will get this far north. It was Colombia’s counterattacking that first paid dividends in the 21st minute. Rodríguez’s initial touch in midfield was far from his best, setting off a scramble that was seized upon by Jefferson Lerma. The Crystal Palace man prodded the ball forward, eventually finding its way to Gustavo Puerta at the edge of the box. Puerta’s curling effort appeared destined for the top corner, but a flying save from Gregor Kobel denied him. Switzerland had a golden opportunity of their own nine minutes later. Daniel Muñoz’s attempted clearance from his own penalty area was blocked by Dan Ndoye, eventually falling to Fabian Rieder rushing in on goal. The attacker’s effort required a fine save from Camilo Vargas, who had to make a similar diving stop to his left to deny Ndoye a couple minutes later. Switzerland brought on Djibril Sow at half-time in place of Ardon Jashari, and the substitute nearly had an immediate impact, firing his effort from an Ndoye cross over the bar just a couple minutes after the second half began. Colombia let another huge chance slip in the 63rd minute, as a loose pass out of the back left Xhaka stranded, his pocket picked by Luis Suárez. With an open look on goal, the striker got his shot all wrong as it sailed harmlessly high and wide. The crowd roared, first in frustration, then with encouragement. These were much‑needed signs of life. Colombia became slightly more dynamic in attack with the removal of Rodríguez for Juan Quintero in the 66th minute. The 34-year-old received a standing ovation upon his exit. Yet despite a more active frontline and slightly more momentum to close the 90 minutes, Colombia’s struggles continued in front of goal. Through balls were an inch or two off. Legs and torsos strayed offside. Breakaways foiled by a stout Swiss defense. Extra time it was, and that’s when Colombia took control. Los Cafeteros were denied a penalty in the 93rd minute, with the substitute Jaminton Campaz tripped by Miro Muheim after getting a touch in the box. Five minutes later, a moment of agony for Colombia, as Jhon Lucumí’s header from Quintero’s pinpoint corner kick rattled the crossbar. Soon after, an audacious long-range strike from Campaz required a smart save from Kobel. The Swiss countered with their own heart-in-throat moment, with Zeki Amdouni, just on a moment earlier, snatched at a loose ball in the box and forced a quality save from Vargas, low and to his left. Soon, one was forced to consider the possibility of a hex placed on the Swiss goalmouth, as a wide-open Campaz fired over the bar from 10 yards out in the 116th minute. Unfortunately for Colombia, the hex remained in effect.
chineseTitle: 瑞士点球淘汰哥伦比亚晋级世界杯四分之一决赛
chineseSummary: 在与哥伦比亚互交白卷后，瑞士在点球大战中以 4-3 获胜，鲁本·瓦尔加斯 (Ruben Vargas) 攻入制胜点球，为与阿根廷的比赛奠定了基础。
chineseContent: 达文森·桑切斯望向天空。库乔·埃尔南德斯艰难地回到队友身边。最后，哥伦比亚队的其他队员都在草地上跳舞，而瑞士队则在黄色的海洋中翩翩起舞，几乎独自一人。瑞士队在点球大战中以 4-3 获胜，为世界杯 16 强赛上两个多小时的紧张、试探性和可笑的无进球足球画上句号。瑞士队自 1954 年以来首次晋级四分之一决赛，当时该阶段是由瑞士主办、共有 16 支球队参加的世界杯淘汰赛的第一场。他们将面临一项艰巨的任务，要取得更好的成绩，四天后将在堪萨斯城迎战梅西和阿根廷队。与其说是一场足球比赛，不如说是一场国际象棋比赛，哥伦比亚和瑞士在 120 多分钟的时间里进行了同样的探索和刺激，各自寻找难以捉摸的突破。控球率保持平衡。中场球员轮流控制比赛进程，但每次只能持续几分钟。有时，这些中场会被完全排除，因为长传球会被用来测试后防线的能力。虽然没有烟火表演，但最后却充满了戏剧性。这场比赛肯定受益于瑞士新星约翰·曼赞比的最后一击，他是球队的头号得分手，但在训练中膝盖受伤，没有出现在球队名单中。哥伦比亚继续通过詹姆斯·罗德里格斯进行进攻。在不列颠哥伦比亚省一个温暖而阳光明媚的日子里，BC Place 独特的屋顶仍然关闭，空调系统似乎有点过分了。在最后一场比赛中，这个场地以及整个加拿大的空气中弥漫着浓浓的湿气，导致共同主办国无法参加本届比赛。这些情况并没有抑制喧闹的人群，毫无疑问，哪一个是最热闹的？
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
